text: I see Joan more as a friend than a co-worker.
Do you think we're huge stars?
I wouldn't say we're huge stars, not yet.
Beautiful and sort of powerful message that...
about queer joy.
Being producers on this film, what was that like, and why was it important for that to be part of your experience?
It was kind of amazing, because it was very new for us both, so we were like...
Suddenly sort of thrust into this experience of actually being able to read all of the iterations of the script
and having actual input on where our characters were going,
not just our characters but the overall piece.
So I think it allowed us both to be sort of a creative input,
but also a mouthpiece basically for the cast as a whole,
which was great.
Because all of the cast care about these characters and the story and the project in general.
I felt very like we'd grown up, and then okay, it's not, and it felt like we'd almost earned it through the three seasons of.
And then yeah, I think that we did good things
Credit to...
but I think we actually did, we put some effort in it.
I think we took it seriously.
And what was it about being a film, why did you think it was important to end the Heart Stopper story on a film?
I think it means that there's something really nice about having a cinematic ending,
and lasering in focus on these two characters, and giving them a more polished...
filming a film means you have more.
Time to make it look nicer, and all these things that in TV, it's you go bam bam bam bam,
whereas the film, you have not a lot of time, but slightly more time to focus on those things, which is really gives them a more picture-perfect ending.
Yeah.
And I also think that in terms of the content of the film and of the sort of final chapter,
I think it works really well as a film, because it's these sort of final...
We've been through so much with these characters, and we've sort of watched them work so many things out, and it feels like this is the sort of final moment.
It's a short period of their life, and yet it's very crucial.
So, I think instead of sort of basically dragging it out along four hours,
it feels like it's a lot more sort of immediate and urgent, and it feels as urgent as it...
Would fit these characters in the moment.
And you mentioned the importance of the content there.
I think, throughout especially the third series and then into the film, that real political nature ramps up,
and I think it does, the content grows and becomes more mature as you guys mature.
So why do you think it was so important to address the themes that you did in the film?
Because it's Heartstopper, I think, because Heartstopper, in its very...
Existence has been this beautiful and sort of powerful message
that about queer joy and about queer humanity,
and these sort of beautiful characters who happen to be queer
and happen to be incredibly happy about it,
and it's how they can live their life the way that they want to live it, and in the best possible way.
So I think that, again, I keep saying, wherever we are in the world,
and whatever the sort of powers that be are doing, and whatever the The zeitgeist,
it feels like making a show like Heartstopper or a film like Heartstopper will always be important,
because it's just a part of life, it's a huge part of life.
Yeah, what is it?
And you guys have all grown up together on set and gone through this massive change
of either having no acting careers or smaller parts, to being huge stars.
What's it been like, for the pair of you but also the other cast members who have gone through the same experience?
Do you think we're huge stars?
I wouldn't say we're huge stars.
Not yet.
Big stars.
One day.
I mean, for me, Heartstopper is the reason I have a career, and touch wood, hopefully will for a while, for a little bit anyway.
And so I always think about what my life would be like if I hadn't done Heartstopper.
I would be just graduated university out of, yeah.
And I think a lot of us feel quite similarly in that gratefulness to the show for allowing us to live our dreams, basically.
Yeah, it's very special.
And what's it like to go through that process of, it was your first acting role, to now being something that you're so well known for?
It must be life changing.
Yeah, completely.
And There's not really.
People always ask, the goal, how's it changed your life?
For me, the biggest question is how it hasn't changed my life, because it's changed my life in pretty much every way.
And I think it's changed all of our lives in so many ways, even though Kit was already a star before Hot Stuff.
Up wasn't a star.
Absolutely not.
But I think your life has changed a lot.
Absolutely, because of it.
Yeah, completely.
Yeah, I mean, it's one of the biggest things that will ever happen to any of us,
just in terms of the sheer difference between what it was before and what it was after.
Yeah.
Yeah.
And I think that 2026 has been such a good year for emerging talent, especially in young adult spaces.
Having watched Heated Rivalry and Off Campus this year, it's been the making of kind of new stars.
And Joe, this is probably something that you can talk to, but why is it so important that you give kind of new actors a chance, especially on these huge streaming platforms?
I mean, I think this industry is still sort of run in a very closed doors way.
And there's so much talent out there, and people out there that, open calls and finding ways to open up industry to more people,
I think is important, because you can't create a content that reflects the outside world if you're not letting the outside world in.
And do you think that now that you have been producers, this is something that you'd like to explore and kind of champion more in the industry?
Definitely.
Yeah, yeah.
I mean, I think that it was clear that, in this case,
obviously we care about this story and these characters greatly and we know them very well,
but I think that there are plenty of stories that we want to tell.
And what's it like being known as a collective pair?
I'm guessing you're really good friends, but is it something where you want to work together in the future?
Yeah, definitely.
I think, regardless if we do or not work together in the future, we'll always be linked and that's really nice.
Yeah, if anything I actually find that I sort of value Joe as a friend.
I see Joe more as a friend than a co-worker.
And so I'm sort of, yeah, if we work together again that's great, but that's it.
I don't really see, obviously he's a great actor and I love watching his work, but I don't really see him as an actor first, I see him as a friend and then.
I think people will probably avoid putting us together for a long time.
Well, I think people will try to probably put us back together.
That's absolutely true, yeah.
I think I've already had meetings of people being like, "What have you..."
I want to know your favourite memory from filming on set for the film.
It's in this documentary that Netflix is releasing.
It's actually in that, so spoilers.
But there's a moment when we were filming season three.
We were filming the fair day, and we snuck on the Helter Skelter that was on, and it was just so fun and sweet.
And watching it back, because Toby had his vlogging camera that he would film loads of stuff on, and lots of that's in the documentary.
So we were just giggling and on this Helter Skelter where we shouldn't have been, literally being naughty school kids, and that was a very special memory.
Yeah, and I think on the film it was probably,
I had a really lovely day when we were in Home Bay.
It was four years to the day from when we had shot at that beach for season one.
So it was very sort of full circle, and we shot a really beautiful scene at the beginning of the day,
and then just sort of spent the rest of the day wandering around Han Bay shooting sweet moments.
So it was a lovely, lovely day, I think.
